capabilities.description: Clarify goals, affected subjects, constraints, vocabulary, and acceptance boundaries before solution work.
fallback: Record assumptions and stop decisions that depend on unanswered questions.
validation: Confirm unresolved questions and acceptance boundaries with the responsible subject.

capabilities.description: Turn an agreed problem into a reviewable product or technical decision document.
fallback: Keep the document in draft and identify missing decision authority.
validation: Review scope, outcomes, constraints, alternatives, and acceptance evidence.

capabilities.description: Run consequential work through a spec-first loop: clarify outcome, separate facts from assumptions, define acceptance, decompose slices, choose implementation path, and verify closure.
fallback: Use native reasoning with a lightweight acceptance checklist when the task is small or already scoped.
validation: Acceptance criteria, assumptions, work slices, selected capability path, and final verification are traceable.

capabilities.description: Decompose an approved outcome into bounded, independently verifiable work slices.
fallback: Retain the larger outcome and mark decomposition gaps instead of inventing tasks.
validation: Check that each slice has inputs, outputs, dependencies, and observable completion.

capabilities.description: Design system boundaries, responsibilities, interactions, and evolution constraints.
fallback: Use the smallest reversible boundary change and document uncertainty.
validation: Review the design against current domain language, decisions, and operational constraints.

capabilities.description: Design stable interfaces and contracts between components or subjects.
fallback: Expose the narrowest reversible interface and defer unsupported flexibility.
validation: Evaluate alternatives against callers, failure modes, compatibility, and testability.

capabilities.description: Model data entities, invariants, ownership, retention, and change semantics.
fallback: Compose requirements and architecture modeling, then avoid irreversible schema commitments until accountable review.
validation: Require domain and storage experts to review invariants and lifecycle assumptions.

capabilities.description: Design accessible, usable, responsive user-facing interactions and visual structure.
fallback: Provide an implementation-neutral interaction specification and preserve design uncertainty.
validation: Probe the live environment for an authorized design workflow and verify the rendered result.

capabilities.description: Implement server-side behavior, integrations, persistence boundaries, and failure handling.
fallback: Implement a smaller reversible slice and report unavailable execution support.
validation: Run repository-defined tests, static checks, and contract verification.

capabilities.description: Develop behavior through observable red, green, and refactor cycles.
fallback: Add the smallest reproducible regression test before changing behavior.
validation: Capture a failing test before the minimal implementation and rerun it after refactoring.

capabilities.description: Choose risk-proportionate test layers, evidence, environments, and quality gates.
fallback: Prioritize critical-path integration checks and disclose untested risks.
validation: Trace each material risk to an executable check and an evidence owner.

capabilities.description: Review changes for correctness, standards, maintainability, and unintended effects.
fallback: Narrow the review scope and state which change surfaces remain unreviewed.
validation: Inspect the bounded change set and verify actionable findings against repository evidence.

capabilities.description: Assess trust boundaries, attack paths, vulnerabilities, and mitigation evidence.
fallback: Perform a bounded manual threat review and recommend specialist review for unresolved risk.
validation: Probe for an authorized security workflow and validate findings from source to impact.

capabilities.description: Determine lawful purpose, minimization, access, retention, and accountable handling of sensitive data.
fallback: Minimize collection and pause processing that lacks a valid authority decision.
validation: Obtain approval from the accountable privacy or legal authority before consequential processing.

capabilities.description: Define budgets and measure latency, throughput, responsiveness, and resource use.
fallback: Report the measurement boundary and avoid unsupported performance claims.
validation: Compare reproducible measurements with an explicit budget or baseline.

capabilities.description: Design actionable logs, metrics, traces, alerts, and service-level evidence.
fallback: Add bounded diagnostic evidence for the critical path and document blind spots.
validation: Verify that critical behaviors and failures are visible without exposing restricted data.

capabilities.description: Automate repeatable integration, quality gates, packaging, and staged delivery.
fallback: Provide a reproducible manual gate sequence without claiming automated delivery.
validation: Run the delivery workflow in its governed environment and inspect gate evidence.

capabilities.description: Make a bounded release decision from readiness, rollout, monitoring, and rollback evidence.
fallback: Hold the release and identify the smallest missing readiness evidence.
validation: Confirm explicit go or no-go authority, acceptance evidence, monitoring, and recovery readiness.

capabilities.description: Replace or retire systems and contracts through measured compatibility and adoption stages.
fallback: Preserve compatibility and delay irreversible removal until evidence is sufficient.
validation: Verify compatibility, adoption, removal gates, and affected-subject communication.

capabilities.description: Restore a known-safe state or service objective after a failed change or incident.
fallback: Stop further change, contain impact, and escalate to the accountable operator.
validation: Exercise the recovery path and verify state integrity plus restored service objectives.

capabilities.description: Reproduce, isolate, explain, and regression-test faults or unexpected behavior.
fallback: Minimize the reproduction and report competing hypotheses with evidence gaps.
validation: Confirm the causal explanation with instrumentation and a passing regression test.

capabilities.description: Classify incoming work by impact, urgency, evidence, ownership, and next state.
fallback: Keep the issue uncommitted to a solution and request missing evidence.
validation: Confirm classification and ownership against the issue lifecycle policy.

capabilities.description: Keep authoritative documentation aligned with domain language, decisions, and implementation evidence.
fallback: Mark stale or uncertain statements and retain traceable source references.
validation: Cross-check updated documentation with authoritative repository and decision records.

capabilities.description: Capture reusable, source-linked learning without promoting transient state to authority.
fallback: Keep a local evidence note and defer durable publication until reviewed.
validation: Review captured knowledge for provenance, sensitivity, freshness, and retrieval value.

capabilities.description: Identify, classify, prioritize, and retire structural liabilities with bounded value evidence.
fallback: Record the debt with impact and defer mutation until authority and capacity exist.
validation: Tie proposed debt work to observable risk, cost, ownership, and verification.

capabilities.description: Transfer bounded context, evidence, decisions, and next actions between collaborating agents or subjects.
fallback: Provide a minimal evidence-linked status summary and request receiver confirmation.
validation: Verify that the receiver can identify scope, current truth, uncertainties, and allowed next actions.

capabilities.description: Turn outcome feedback into validated improvements to decisions, workflows, and safeguards.
fallback: Record observed outcomes and unresolved causes for later accountable review.
validation: Compare intended and actual outcomes, then assign measurable follow-up changes.

recipes.authorization: Side-effecting implementation, installation, migration, commit, push, release, deployment, or rollback remains governed by the active repository and user authorization.
evidenceGates: Outcome, non-goals, affected subjects, assumptions, and acceptance criteria are separated before implementation.
Work is decomposed into independently verifiable slices and routed to the smallest sufficient native, curated, runtime, or human-confirmation path.
failurePolicy: If ownership, acceptance, or risk is unclear, stop at requirements clarification instead of inventing implementation scope.
If repository commands or authority surfaces are unknown, keep the output as a proposal and do not run side-effecting actions.

steps.when: product, interface, or long-lived behavior needs a durable spec
verification strategy is not already obvious
new or changed behavior is implemented
the work approaches a release boundary

terminalCriteria: The task has acceptance evidence, verified slices, recorded unresolved assumptions, and a clear continuation or closeout state.
recipes.trigger: Consequential or ambiguous work needs a spec-first route before implementation or release

evidenceGates: Boundary goals, callers, failure modes, compatibility expectations, and test surfaces are identified.
At least one candidate contract is reviewed against architecture and code-review capabilities.
failurePolicy: If callers, compatibility needs, or failure modes are unknown, stop at requirements clarification.
If implementation evidence is unavailable, publish only a reversible interface proposal.

steps.when: a candidate contract or implementation exists

terminalCriteria: A narrow contract, known tradeoffs, verification surface, and unresolved assumptions are documented.
recipes.trigger: A component boundary or API needs alternatives evaluated without a dedicated duplicate Skill

recipes.authorization: Irreversible schema, migration, retention, and regulated-data decisions remain subject to their accountable human authorities.
evidenceGates: Entities, invariants, ownership, retention, lifecycle semantics, and change risks are named.
Privacy or regulated-data implications are evaluated when the model touches sensitive data.
failurePolicy: If authority or data sensitivity is unclear, stop before schema, migration, or retention commitments.
If no reviewable schema or contract exists, keep the output as a model proposal.

steps.when: personal, confidential, regulated, or retention-sensitive data is involved
a schema, contract, or migration candidate exists

terminalCriteria: The model has reviewable invariants, owners, retention assumptions, and reversible next steps.
recipes.trigger: A system needs explicit entities, invariants, ownership, retention, and change semantics without adding an overlapping dedicated Skill

evidenceGates: Material risks are mapped to test layers, owners, commands, environments, and acceptance evidence.
Performance, security, and delivery gates are included when their risk conditions apply.
failurePolicy: If risk ownership or execution environment is missing, identify the unverified risks instead of claiming coverage.
If an automated gate is unavailable, provide the smallest reproducible manual check.

steps.when: behavior is being added or corrected
a performance objective or regression risk exists
a trust boundary or material threat changes

terminalCriteria: Each critical risk has an executable or explicitly deferred check with a stated evidence owner.
recipes.trigger: A change needs risk-proportionate verification across test layers and delivery gates

evidenceGates: Purpose, minimization, redaction, retention, access, and trust-boundary assumptions are explicit.
Telemetry fields and destinations are reviewed before collection or export.
failurePolicy: If privacy authority or data classification is missing, stop before adding or exporting telemetry.
If security review is unavailable for a new boundary, keep evidence local and bounded.

steps.when: purpose, minimization, redaction, retention, and access are defined
telemetry crosses a new trust boundary

terminalCriteria: Telemetry captures the critical behavior while minimizing sensitive exposure and documenting blind spots.
recipes.trigger: Operational evidence may contain personal, confidential, regulated, or secret data

recipes.authorization: Every merge, publication, release, deployment, migration, or rollback remains separately authorized.
evidenceGates: Test, observability, performance, delivery, rollback, and release-readiness evidence are available or explicitly waived.
Go or no-go authority and affected-subject communication path are identified.
failurePolicy: If release authority or rollback evidence is missing, hold the release.
If any gate is unavailable, record the smallest missing evidence and do not imply readiness.
terminalCriteria: A go, no-go, or hold decision is recorded with readiness evidence, monitoring, and recovery posture.
recipes.trigger: A change is approaching a governed release boundary

evidenceGates: Compatibility, adoption, observability, rollback, and release-readiness evidence are present.
Affected consumers and removal gates are named.
failurePolicy: If consumers or rollback path are unknown, preserve compatibility and defer removal.
If adoption evidence is incomplete, continue migration support rather than cutting over.
terminalCriteria: Migration can proceed, pause, or retire only with documented adoption, compatibility, and rollback state.
recipes.trigger: An interface, dependency, feature, data contract, or system is replaced or retired

recipes.authorization: Production rollback and destructive recovery actions require the accountable operator's explicit authorization.
evidenceGates: Current impact, known-safe target, observability signals, and accountable operator are identified.
Regression evidence is planned when recovery behavior changes.
failurePolicy: If operator authority is missing, contain impact and stop before destructive recovery.
If the safe target is unknown, preserve evidence and escalate instead of guessing.

steps.when: safe restoration does not explain the cause

terminalCriteria: Service or state objective is restored, contained, or explicitly escalated with remaining risk recorded.
recipes.trigger: A failed change or incident requires controlled restoration of a known-safe state

evidenceGates: Learning is tied to source evidence, sensitivity review, freshness, and expected retrieval value.
Authoritative documentation changes are separated from local notes and handoff material.
failurePolicy: If provenance or sensitivity is unclear, keep the note local and do not promote it to authority.
If no future consumer is known, capture a minimal source-linked summary instead of expanding process.

steps.when: learning changes an authoritative standard or operating path
another subject will continue the work

terminalCriteria: Reusable learning is captured, deliberately deferred, or handed off with provenance and authority boundaries.
recipes.trigger: A completed decision, delivery, incident, or experiment produced reusable learning

relations.condition: durable behavior or product intent needs specification
the work reaches a launch boundary
behavior must be measurable before optimization
a release changes or removes an existing contract
an issue is accepted as a reproducible fault

skills.description: Use when designing or modifying CI/CD pipelines, automated quality gates, test runners, staged delivery, or deployment safeguards; not for diagnosing an already-failing GitHub Actions run.
skills.name: ci-cd-and-automation

skills.description: Use when replacing, retiring, or consolidating systems, APIs, features, dependencies, or data contracts and a measured compatibility, migration, or removal plan is required.
skills.name: deprecation-and-migration

skills.description: Disciplined diagnosis loop for hard bugs and performance regressions. Reproduce → minimise → hypothesise → instrument → fix → regression-test. Use when user says "diagnose this" / "debug this", reports a bug, says something is broken/throwing/failing, or describes a performance regression.
skills.name: diagnose

skills.description: Set up hooks to block dangerous git commands (push, reset --hard, clean, branch -D, etc.) before they execute. Works in any agent environment or manual terminal workflow. Use when the user wants to prevent destructive git operations or add git safety guardrails.
skills.name: git-guardrails

skills.description: Grilling session that challenges your plan against the existing domain model, sharpens terminology, and updates documentation (CONTEXT.md, ADRs) inline as decisions crystallise. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan against their project's language and documented decisions.
skills.name: grill-with-docs

skills.description: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.
skills.name: handoff

skills.description: Find deepening opportunities in a codebase, informed by the domain language in CONTEXT.md and the decisions in docs/adr/. Use when the user wants to improve architecture, find refactoring opportunities, consolidate tightly-coupled modules, or make a codebase more testable and AI-navigable.
skills.name: improve-codebase-architecture

skills.description: Replace unsafe `as` type assertions in TypeScript test files with type-safe alternatives. Use when user wants to replace `as` in tests, pass partial test data safely, or test with intentionally wrong types without losing autocomplete.
skills.name: migrate-to-shoehorn

skills.description: Use when production behavior is not sufficiently visible, or when adding or reviewing logs, metrics, traces, alerts, service-level indicators, retries, queues, jobs, or external integrations.
skills.name: observability-and-instrumentation

skills.description: Use when defining a performance budget, measuring a known performance concern, or optimizing latency, throughput, resource use, load time, responsiveness, or scalability after evidence identifies a target or bottleneck.
skills.name: performance-optimization

skills.description: Build a throwaway prototype to flesh out a design before committing to it. Routes between two branches — a runnable terminal app for state/business-logic questions, or several radically different UI variations toggleable from one route. Use when the user wants to prototype, sanity-check a data model or state machine, mock up a UI, explore design options, or says "prototype this", "let me play with it", "try a few designs".
skills.name: prototype

skills.description: Review the changes since a fixed point (commit, branch, tag, or merge-base) along two axes — Standards (does the code follow this repo's documented coding standards?) and Spec (does the code match what the originating issue/PRD asked for?). Runs both reviews in parallel sub-agents and reports them side by side. Use when the user wants to review a branch, a PR, work-in-progress changes, or asks to "review since X".
skills.name: review

skills.description: Sets up an `## Agent skills` block in the repo's agent config file and `docs/agents/` so engineering skills know this repo's issue tracker (GitHub, GitLab, or local markdown), triage label vocabulary, and domain doc layout. Run before first use of `to-issues`, `to-prd`, `triage`, `diagnose`, `tdd`, `improve-codebase-architecture`, or `zoom-out` — or if those skills appear to be missing context about the issue tracker, triage labels, or domain docs.
skills.name: setup-project-skills

skills.description: Use when preparing a production launch, staged rollout, migration, beta, release-readiness decision, monitoring plan, rollback plan, or post-launch verification; not for ordinary Git branch publication.
skills.name: shipping-and-launch

skills.description: Test-driven development with red-green-refactor loop. Use when user wants to build features or fix bugs using TDD, mentions "red-green-refactor", wants integration tests, or asks for test-first development.
skills.name: tdd

skills.description: Break a plan, spec, or PRD into independently-grabbable issues on the project issue tracker using tracer-bullet vertical slices. Use when user wants to convert a plan into issues, create implementation tickets, or break down work into issues.
skills.name: to-issues

skills.description: Turn the current conversation context into a PRD and publish it to the project issue tracker. Use when user wants to create a PRD from the current context.
skills.name: to-prd

skills.description: Triage issues through a state machine driven by triage roles. Use when user wants to create an issue, triage issues, review incoming bugs or feature requests, prepare issues for an AFK agent, or manage issue workflow.
skills.name: triage

skills.description: Extract a DDD-style ubiquitous language glossary from the current conversation, flagging ambiguities and proposing canonical terms. Saves to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md. Use when user wants to define domain terms, build a glossary, harden terminology, create a ubiquitous language, or mentions "domain model" or "DDD".
skills.name: ubiquitous-language
